zones.name: Analysis Engine
zones.description: Core analysis pipeline implementing zone enrichment, context generation, callgraph findings, and AI-assisted classification.
insights: 16 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
With 16 entry points this zone has a wide public surface — consider whether all are truly public API or if some should be internal
High cohesion (0.66) confirms this zone is well-clustered around a single domain responsibility
The claude-client.ts inside this zone suggests a direct LLM dependency — verify it routes through @n-dx/llm-client rather than calling Anthropic APIs directly
High cohesion (0.66) and low coupling (0.34) indicate a well-bounded analysis domain — this is the healthiest zone in the batch.
16 entry points is unusually broad for a single zone; audit whether all exports need to be public or if the public API surface can be narrowed.
claude-client.ts should proxy through @n-dx/llm-client to maintain the four-tier dependency hierarchy and avoid direct vendor coupling.
Zone "analysis-engine" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The 12 outgoing imports from analysis-engine into cli-operations constitute a layer inversion — domain analysis code must not depend on CLI modules; this is the most severe structural violation in the batch

analysis-engine → cli-operations (12 imports): domain layer importing from CLI layer is a hard layer inversion; analysis services must not depend on CLI command modules — extract shared types or utilities downward to break this coupling

enrich-config.ts is classified as [config] inside analysis-engine, mixing pipeline configuration with domain service logic in the same zone — configuration for the enrichment pipeline should be co-located with other config artifacts or extracted to prevent config churn from touching the domain zone

The 16-entry-point surface combined with files spanning 9 directories means analysis-engine's public API cannot be audited by reading a single public.ts or index.ts — callers must know internal paths to import correctly, defeating the purpose of a zone boundary

enrich-config.ts (classified [config]) is co-located with domain services in analysis-engine; configuration files that change on deployment cadence should not share a zone with algorithmic services that change on feature cadence — extract to a dedicated config layer to decouple change rates

16 entry points spanning 9 directories means there is no canonical import path into this zone; consumers import from arbitrary internal paths, making any file move a breaking change — consolidate exports behind a single index or public.ts to create a stable zone API surface
completion-reader.ts handles streaming I/O concerns at the same zone level as algorithmic services — an implicit I/O abstraction layer is missing between the LLM client and the analysis pipeline

The zone name 'analysis-engine' implies a single engine, but files span 9 directories with 16 entry points — the name sets a false expectation of bounded scope that misleads consumers about the zone's actual surface area

completion-reader.ts handles streaming completion I/O while its sibling files perform domain analysis — extract streaming/I/O adapters into a dedicated adapter layer below the analysis services to give I/O concerns a stable, separately evolvable home

The zone name 'analysis-engine' is singular and bounded but the zone spans 9 directories with 16 entry points; rename or split the zone so the name truthfully describes its scope — a misleading name causes developers to underestimate blast radius when modifying files here
[call graph] 998 internal calls, 94 outgoing, 210 incoming (cohesion: 0.91, coupling: 0.09)

zones.name: Branch Work Analysis
zones.description: Schema foundation, branch-work tracking pipeline, risk scoring, and MCP server wiring grouped by import proximity to the core schema types.
insights: High coupling (0.58) — 30 imports target "analysis-engine"
The zone name 'unit' is misleading — it conflates schema types, branch-work production code, and test helpers, which should be separated
Coupling of 0.58 is near the warning threshold and reflects this zone's role as a schema hub depended on by many other zones
The test helper file tests/unit/analyzers/zones-helpers.ts being grouped with production code indicates the zone boundary is driven by import edges rather than domain intent
Coupling at 0.58 approaches the warning threshold (>0.6); monitor as new consumers of schema/v1.ts and data-files.ts are added.
The zone mixes schema foundation files (v1.ts, data-files.ts), branch-work services, and a test helper — low cohesion (0.42) reflects this mixed responsibility.
Extracting schema/v1.ts and schema/data-files.ts into a dedicated schema-foundation zone would reduce coupling and clarify the dependency graph for downstream consumers.
Zone "branch-work-analysis" has files across 11 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Schema types v1.ts and data-files.ts residing inside branch-work-analysis act as a foundation layer consumed by 3+ zones, but inherit the coupling and mixed-content problems of their host zone — they have no zone-level isolation of their own

The 30 outgoing imports from branch-work-analysis into analysis-engine combined with 7 return imports makes this pair the highest-traffic bidirectional coupling in the graph, accounting for 37 of the total cross-zone edges

Schema foundation types (v1.ts, schema/index.ts) are co-located inside branch-work-analysis rather than a dedicated foundation zone; consumers importing schema types inadvertently couple to the branch-work-analysis zone boundary, making schema extraction riskier over time

branch-work-analysis ↔ analysis-engine (30+7 crossings) is the dominant edge pair in the graph — 37 cross-zone imports between two zones signals that zone boundaries do not reflect real module seams; these zones likely share a common abstraction that should be extracted

mcp.ts (a CLI command entry point) is co-located with domain analysis services inside branch-work-analysis — a CLI adapter living inside a domain zone violates the CLI-above-domain principle at the intra-zone level, independent of cross-zone import direction
pr-markdown-template.ts is a presentation/templating concern embedded in a domain analysis zone — templates are output-layer artifacts and should live in cli-operations or a dedicated rendering layer

workspace-aggregate.ts (aggregation service) and workspace.ts (CLI command) being in the same zone means domain aggregation logic and its CLI adapter share a zone boundary, making it impossible to evolve either independently without risking the other

mcp.ts is a CLI command co-located with domain services in branch-work-analysis; CLI adapters must not share a zone boundary with domain logic they adapt — move mcp.ts to cli-operations to enforce CLI-above-domain layering within the zone, not just across zones

pr-markdown-template.ts is a presentation-layer template embedded in a domain analysis zone; output templates have no domain responsibility and should reside in cli-operations or a dedicated output-rendering zone to prevent domain logic from acquiring a dependency on formatting details

branch-work-analysis has BOTH below-midpoint cohesion (0.42) AND above-midpoint coupling (0.58) simultaneously — per the severity guide this combination defines a fragile zone; it is the only zone in this codebase that satisfies both fragility conditions at once

The cross-zone import table uses the alias 'unit' for this zone while the canonical zone ID is 'branch-work-analysis' — the alias persists from a prior zone name and creates documentation ambiguity when tracing import edges across analysis passes

workspace-aggregate.ts (domain service) and the CLI's workspace.ts share a 'workspace' naming prefix across two different zones with no enforced naming convention — the shared prefix implies a module family but the files are governed by different zone rules

branch-work-analysis is the only zone with both cohesion below 0.5 and coupling above 0.5 — treat it as the highest-fragility zone in the codebase; prioritize extracting schema foundation types first (reduces coupling) and moving CLI artifacts out second (increases cohesion) before adding any new files to this zone

The 'unit' alias used in cross-import tables for this zone is a residual name from a prior zone structure; update all documentation and tooling references to use the canonical ID 'branch-work-analysis' to prevent import tracing errors in future analysis passes

workspace-aggregate.ts and workspace.ts (CLI) share a naming prefix implying a module family, but are governed by different zone boundaries with no enforced convention; document an explicit naming rule — domain aggregates use '-aggregate' suffix, CLI adapters use the bare noun — and verify all paired domain/CLI files follow it

zones.name: Claude Config
zones.description: Stores Claude Code editor settings and local overrides for the workspace.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 2 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
These files are editor-specific and should be reviewed for accidental secret exposure in settings.local.json
No entry points or imports mean this zone is fully isolated — correct for config-only files
Consider adding settings.local.json to .gitignore if it contains developer-specific overrides
Zone contains only editor configuration — zero cohesion and coupling scores are expected and correct for this role.
settings.local.json may contain developer-specific overrides that shouldn't be committed; verify .gitignore excludes it.

zones.name: CLI Operations
zones.description: CLI command implementations, output formatting, error handling, and credential/git helpers that form the user-facing interface of sourcevision.
insights: Good cohesion (0.67) shows CLI commands are tightly grouped around their shared output and error infrastructure
The prd-epic-resolver.ts inside a sourcevision CLI zone suggests a cross-domain concern that may belong in rex or an integration layer
cli → unit (8 imports) and unit → cli (4 imports) form a bidirectional dependency that should be broken by extracting shared types to a lower layer
Zone "cli-operations" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
prd-epic-resolver.ts references PRD/epic concepts inside the sourcevision CLI zone — verify this doesn't create a hidden runtime dependency on rex.
Bidirectional imports between cli and unit zones (8 + 4 cross-zone imports) indicate a cycle risk; shared types or utilities should be extracted downward.
High cohesion (0.67) confirms CLI commands are correctly organized around shared output and error infrastructure.

cli-operations is both a dependent of and a dependency for analysis-engine (5 outgoing + 12 incoming), placing it in a topologically ambiguous position — it cannot be cleanly above or below analysis-engine in any layered hierarchy

The prd-epic-resolver.ts file in cli-operations introduces a silent PRD domain concept into the sourcevision CLI; if this file imports from rex at runtime it creates an undeclared cross-package dependency that bypasses the gateway pattern

cli-operations receives 12 imports from analysis-engine while also sending 5 imports back — bidirectional coupling with a domain layer means cli-operations contains logic that the domain needs, which should instead live in a shared utility or types layer

prd-epic-resolver.ts references PRD/epic domain concepts inside the sourcevision CLI zone; audit whether this file imports from rex at runtime, which would violate the four-tier dependency hierarchy and the gateway pattern without a declared gateway module

branch-work-collector.ts is classified as [service] inside cli-operations despite its name and likely responsibility aligning with branch-work-analysis domain — misplacement here is a plausible root cause of some of the 12 analysis-engine→cli-operations imports: if analysis-engine needs branch-work collection logic it must reach into cli-operations to get it, creating the layer inversion

manifest.ts resides in cli-operations yet manifests are analysis pipeline outputs consumed by analysis-engine; if analysis-engine imports manifest.ts this single misplaced file alone may account for multiple of the 12 cross-layer imports driving the critical layer inversion finding

branch-work-collector.ts lives in cli-operations but collects domain data for branch-work-analysis; moving it to branch-work-analysis would eliminate a cross-layer import forcing analysis-engine to reach into the CLI zone for domain data collection logic

manifest.ts is an analysis pipeline artifact (analysis-engine output) placed inside cli-operations; if analysis-engine imports it, this single misplacement is a direct cause of the critical layer inversion — move manifest.ts into analysis-engine or a shared types layer

git-credential-helper.ts is a security-sensitive credential operation co-located with general CLI commands in cli-operations — credential helpers have a distinct threat model (secret exposure, process isolation) and should be isolated behind an abstraction or placed in a dedicated security-utilities zone

export-pdf.ts introduces a binary output dependency (PDF generation) into cli-operations alongside text-output commands — binary rendering has a different dependency profile and failure mode than text CLI commands, and mixing them inflates the zone's external dependency surface silently

git-credential-helper.ts handles credential secrets inside the same zone as general CLI output commands; isolate it behind a CredentialHelper interface or move it to a dedicated security-utilities zone so that secret-handling logic has an explicit, auditable boundary and cannot be accidentally imported by non-security callers

export-pdf.ts introduces a binary rendering dependency (PDF) into cli-operations alongside text-output commands; if PDF generation pulls in a heavyweight renderer dependency, extract it to a separate output-renderers zone to prevent that dependency from being loaded for all CLI consumers that never use PDF export

zones.name: File Analysis Pipeline
zones.description: Houses the four concrete analyzer implementations (callgraph, components, imports, inventory) that form the sequential analysis pipeline, alongside path utilities and route detection.
insights: High coupling (0.52) — 15 imports target "analysis-engine"
The four core analyzer services map cleanly to the four analysis phases, which is good separation of concern within this zone.
Route detection is co-located with the file analyzers rather than the zone clustering code, which is appropriate since it operates on individual file paths.
Bidirectional imports with the Analysis Engine zone (15 imports out, 7 in) suggest these two zones are tightly coupled and may benefit from consolidation into a single zone.

Bidirectional coupling with the Analysis Engine zone (analyzers-2 → analyzers: 15, analyzers → analyzers-2: 7) indicates the algorithmic boundary between these two zones does not match the intended module boundary — consider treating them as one cohesive analysis layer.

src/util/paths.ts is listed as an entrypoint, suggesting it is imported by multiple external zones; if path utilities are widely shared, they may belong in a dedicated utility zone rather than alongside the analyzer services.

Cohesion (0.48) and coupling (0.52) are both near the midpoint, reflecting a zone that is neither fully self-contained nor excessively open — acceptable for a pipeline stage but worth monitoring as the analyzer count grows.

The 15 outgoing imports from this zone to analysis-engine versus only 7 returning makes this zone a net consumer — it depends heavily on analysis-engine abstractions rather than providing them, suggesting the dependency direction is correct but the interface boundary is underspecified.

The co-location of src/util/paths.ts with analyzer services creates a false structural signal — path utilities are likely imported by zones outside this pipeline, making them a misclassified shared utility rather than a pipeline-local concern.

src/util/paths.ts should be extracted to a dedicated utility zone; its presence in the file-analysis-pipeline zone obscures its true role as a cross-cutting shared utility and inflates this zone's apparent coupling.

src/analyzers/index.ts is classified as [entrypoint] yet the zone still has 15 outgoing imports to analysis-engine — an entrypoint that does not reduce external coupling is not functioning as an abstraction boundary; consumers are bypassing it and importing implementation files directly.

The [route-handler] archetype assigned to src/analyzers/route-detection.ts is semantically incorrect — route-handler implies HTTP request dispatch, but this file detects file-system routing patterns from file paths; the correct archetype is [utility] or [service], and the mislabel will cause zone-based tooling to misclassify it as application-layer code.

src/analyzers/route-detection.ts carries the [route-handler] archetype but performs static file-path analysis, not HTTP routing; this archetype mislabel causes tooling to misclassify a static analysis utility as an application-layer request handler — reclassify to [utility].

src/analyzers/index.ts is designated [entrypoint] but the zone still exports 15 cross-zone import edges to analysis-engine, meaning the barrel export is not functioning as a stable abstraction boundary — consumers import implementation files directly, rendering the entrypoint ineffective as an isolation mechanism.

src/analyzers/index.ts functions as a barrel re-export but the 15 outgoing cross-zone edges prove consumers bypass it entirely — a non-enforced barrel provides no abstraction value and adds an extra indirection layer with no benefit; it should either be enforced via lint rules or removed to reduce confusion.

The zone mixes [service] and [utility] archetypes without a clear boundary rule: inventory.ts, imports.ts, components.ts, callgraph.ts are [service]; paths.ts is [utility]; route-detection.ts is misclassified as [route-handler]. The presence of three archetypes in one zone is a naming-convention signal that this zone contains at least two separable concerns.

src/analyzers/index.ts barrel export should be either lint-enforced as the only allowed cross-zone import point or deleted — a barrel that consumers bypass provides false encapsulation and adds indirection without isolation benefit.

Three archetypes ([service], [utility], [route-handler]) coexist in this zone with no documented rule governing which archetype belongs here — establish an explicit archetype policy per zone to make misclassifications detectable by tooling.
[call graph] 384 internal calls, 7 outgoing, 34 incoming (cohesion: 0.98, coupling: 0.02)

zones.name: Schema Validation
zones.description: Contains the schema validation logic (src/schema/validate.ts) alongside its unit, integration, and e2e tests, forming a self-contained correctness boundary for analysis output formats.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling (0.0) confirm that schema validation has no runtime dependencies on the rest of the codebase — an ideal property for a validation layer.
Co-locating e2e, integration, and unit tests with the single production file means every layer of the test pyramid is represented for this module.
The zone name 'e2e' is a clustering artifact; the canonical architectural identity is schema validation, which has a broader test scope than the label implies.

src/schema/validate.ts is the sole production file in this zone alongside three test files spanning all test levels — this is a well-tested, isolated module and a positive example of the single-responsibility principle.

The algorithmic zone ID 'e2e' was assigned because the e2e test files dominate by count; ensure CI tooling and zone-based filtering do not inadvertently exclude validate.ts from production code analysis passes.
Zero external coupling means the schema validator can be evolved or replaced without impacting any other zone at runtime — a desirable property worth preserving as schema versions are added.

The schema-validation zone's perfect isolation (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0) combined with src/schema/index.ts and src/schema/v1.ts being imported by 3–4 other zones reveals a structural mismatch — the production schema modules are load-bearing cross-zone infrastructure but are classified in a zone named for validation; this misleads zone-based tooling about where schema types live architecturally.

The schema zone is effectively a hidden foundation layer: other zones depend on it at runtime but it has no outgoing runtime dependencies, which is the correct property for a foundation — however it should be explicitly named and governed as such rather than discovered as a side-effect of test co-location.

src/schema/index.ts and src/schema/v1.ts function as a shared foundation layer (imported by 3–4 zones, zero outgoing imports) but are classified under a zone named 'schema-validation' dominated by test files — rename or restructure to reflect the foundation role and prevent the schema types from being treated as validation-layer internals.

tests/e2e/cli-analyze.test.ts and tests/e2e/cli-serve.test.ts exercise CLI command behavior end-to-end, not schema validation logic; they were co-located here because they import schema types, but their architectural home is the CLI e2e test zone — their presence in schema-validation inflates this zone's apparent test coverage of the schema module and obscures the true scope of CLI e2e testing.

tests/e2e/cli-analyze.test.ts and tests/e2e/cli-serve.test.ts are CLI behavior tests misclassified into the schema-validation zone because they happen to import schema types; this makes the schema zone appear to own CLI e2e coverage and splits CLI test files across two zones — move both files to the CLI e2e test zone.

The zone contains exactly one production file (src/schema/validate.ts) and four test files spanning three test levels; the zone name 'schema-validation' accurately describes only the production file, but the presence of two CLI e2e test files means the zone's centroid in the import graph is pulled toward CLI testing, not schema validation — this naming drift will worsen as more CLI e2e tests are added.

tests/e2e/cli-analyze.test.ts and tests/e2e/cli-serve.test.ts are the only e2e test files visible in this zone's file list; if there is no dedicated CLI e2e zone, these files represent the entirety of CLI e2e coverage — their misclassification into schema-validation means CLI e2e coverage is invisible in any zone-level test coverage report.

Create a dedicated cli-e2e zone and move tests/e2e/cli-analyze.test.ts and tests/e2e/cli-serve.test.ts into it — without a canonical CLI e2e zone, zone-level test coverage reports cannot surface CLI end-to-end test presence, and new CLI e2e tests have no clear zone home.

zones.name: Test Fixtures
zones.description: Static sample projects (a Remix app and a small TypeScript project) that serve as realistic analysis targets for e2e and integration tests.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

The two fixture projects cover distinct technology profiles (framework-based Remix routes vs. plain TypeScript modules), providing good breadth for testing the analyzer across different import graph shapes.
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling (0.0) confirm fixtures are completely isolated from production code, as expected for inert test data.
Fixture files should be excluded from sourcevision's own analysis runs to avoid circular self-analysis artifacts — verify that ignore patterns in the manifest cover tests/fixtures/.

Both fixture projects are realistic enough to exercise route detection, import graph construction, and component cataloging — maintaining at least one framework-based and one plain-TS fixture as the test suite grows will keep coverage representative.
Zero coupling confirms no production file imports from fixtures, which is correct; any accidental runtime import of fixture data would be a test-isolation defect to address immediately.

The Remix fixture includes nested route files (_auth.login.tsx, users.$id.tsx) which are valuable for validating file-system route detection; ensure the route-detection analyzer tests reference this fixture explicitly rather than synthetic data.
Zone "test-fixtures" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The fixture zone's zero coupling confirms no production code accidentally imports test data, but the inverse risk also exists: if fixture projects share file paths with production ignore patterns, the analyzer could silently exclude them from fixture-based e2e validation runs.

Fixture projects spanning 5 directories with no centralized entry point risk being partially excluded by glob-based ignore rules — verify that sourcevision's own manifest ignore patterns do not accidentally suppress fixture files needed for e2e test coverage.

The Remix fixture's route files use file-system-convention naming (_auth.login.tsx, users.$id.tsx) that is only meaningful to the route-detection analyzer; if the route-detection tests do not explicitly reference these fixture files, the naming conventions are untested and may silently break if the route-detection heuristics change.

The small-ts-project fixture contains inter-service imports (user-service.ts likely imports user.ts; email-service.ts likely imports user.ts) creating an internal import graph within the fixture. Sourcevision's own import analyzer tests exercise this fixture, meaning sourcevision is performing meta-analysis — analyzing an import graph specifically designed to be analyzed. If the fixture's internal import structure silently changes, tests may pass with incorrect import graph data.

The Remix fixture files use file-system convention naming (_auth.login.tsx, users.$id.tsx) that encodes routing semantics in the filename; these conventions are specific to Remix v2 file-system routing. If sourcevision adds support for other file-system routers (Next.js App Router, TanStack Router), the single Remix fixture will not exercise the differences between routing conventions — add a second framework fixture or parameterize route-detection tests over multiple fixture projects.

Add a fixture integrity test that asserts the expected internal import graph of small-ts-project — if fixture files are accidentally modified, import graph tests may silently pass with stale expected data rather than failing with a clear fixture-changed error.

Document the Remix fixture's file-system routing convention dependency (Remix v2 _prefix and $ param syntax) in a fixture README or inline comment — undocumented fixture conventions break silently when route-detection heuristics are updated for a different router.

zones.name: Workspace Root
zones.description: Top-level project scaffolding: documentation, monorepo config, TypeScript project references, and build metadata.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 10 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
The presence of tsconfig.tsbuildinfo in this zone suggests incremental build artifacts are committed — typically these should be gitignored
Architecture docs (ARCHITECTURE.md, WORKSPACE_DESIGN.md) being co-located with package.json is appropriate for a monorepo root
vitest.config.ts at the root coordinates cross-package test runs and is a natural entry point for the workspace test suite
tsconfig.tsbuildinfo is a build artifact and should be added to .gitignore to avoid noisy diffs.
Multiple architecture documentation files (ARCHITECTURE.md, WORKSPACE_DESIGN.md, README.md) at the root provide good onboarding context for the monorepo.
Zero coupling is expected here — root config files should not be imported by source code.

zones.name: Zone Detection Engine
zones.description: Implements Louvain community detection, zone hashing, and zone subdivision/size-policy logic to cluster files into stable architectural zones.
insights: Low cohesion (0.29) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.71) — 11 imports target "analysis-engine"
Louvain, zone-hash, and zones.ts form a coherent clustering subsystem, but the low cohesion score suggests one or more files have import patterns that stray outside this subsystem.
Three focused test files (zone-detection, zone-size-policy, zone-subdivision) provide good behavioral coverage of the core clustering decisions.
High coupling to surrounding zones means changes to the zone detection algorithm are likely to ripple across the codebase — stabilizing the public API surface of zones.ts would reduce this risk.

Cohesion of 0.29 is below the 0.4 threshold, indicating that the files grouped here have sparse internal import connections relative to their external ones — zone-hash.ts in particular may be imported more by consumers than by its zone peers.

Coupling of 0.71 exceeds the 0.6 warning threshold; combined with bidirectional imports to both the Analysis Engine and Branch Work Analysis zones, the zone detection subsystem is a high-traffic hub that could become a bottleneck for future refactors.

The Louvain algorithm (louvain.ts) and the zone lifecycle manager (zones.ts) are co-located but serve distinct concerns — algorithm versus orchestration; splitting them into separate modules would improve testability and lower coupling.

With only 6 files and 0.71 coupling, this zone has the worst cohesion-to-size ratio in the codebase — small zones with high coupling are the highest refactoring risk because every file change has a disproportionate cross-zone blast radius.

The three test files (zone-detection, zone-size-policy, zone-subdivision) cover distinct behavioral concerns that map to separate production responsibilities — this test structure implicitly confirms the zone should be split, as each test file likely corresponds to a separable module.

Zone-detection-engine has the worst cohesion/coupling ratio (0.29/0.71) at the smallest meaningful size (6 files) — this combination makes it the highest per-file refactoring risk in the codebase and the most urgent candidate for boundary stabilization.

The 1:1 ratio of test files to production files (3 test files for louvain.ts, zone-hash.ts, zones.ts) provides unusually thorough coverage but also implies each test file couples tightly to a single production file — if the zone is split, the test-to-production mapping will remain clean, which removes one common obstacle to splitting.

zone-hash.ts produces stable deterministic IDs for zones across analysis runs; if output-generation modules (llms.txt, CONTEXT.md writers) consume zone IDs they must also consume zone-hash.ts, making it a shared utility that has been accidentally enclosed inside the detection zone rather than placed in a shared foundation.

zone-hash.ts generates stable zone IDs consumed by both zone detection and output generation phases; enclosing it inside the zone-detection-engine zone forces output writers to import across a zone boundary into the detection subsystem rather than from a shared utility layer — extract to a dedicated utility zone.

louvain.ts is the only file in the entire src/analyzers/ directory named after a specific algorithm rather than a functional responsibility (compare: zones.ts, zone-hash.ts, imports.ts, inventory.ts). If the Louvain algorithm is replaced with another community detection algorithm, the filename becomes a stale implementation detail leak — rename to graph-clustering.ts or community-detection.ts to match the functional naming convention used by all peer files.

The three test files (zone-detection.test.ts, zone-size-policy.test.ts, zone-subdivision.test.ts) cover zones.ts behaviors but there is no dedicated louvain.test.ts — this means the Louvain algorithm is tested only indirectly through zones.ts integration, not in isolation. If the clustering algorithm is replaced or tuned, there is no unit-level regression harness to validate the change.

The zone name 'zone-detection-engine' uses the suffix '-engine' while its primary file is zones.ts (not zone-detection.ts or zone-detection-engine.ts) — the file name and zone name follow different naming conventions, creating a lookup mismatch for developers navigating from zone metadata to source files.

Rename louvain.ts to graph-clustering.ts or community-detection.ts to align with the functional naming convention used by all peer files in src/analyzers/ — algorithm names leak implementation details and become stale when the algorithm changes.

Add a dedicated unit test for the Louvain clustering algorithm in isolation (louvain.test.ts or graph-clustering.test.ts) — the current test suite exercises zones.ts integration but provides no regression harness if the clustering algorithm is swapped or parameterized differently.

Bidirectional coupling: "branch-work-analysis" ↔ "schema-validation" (2+1 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 39 files vs smallest with 2 — uneven decomposition
The analysis-engine zone is the architectural hub with the most entry points and the highest in-degree from other zones — changes here have the widest blast radius
A bidirectional import cycle exists between cli-operations and branch-work-analysis zones; extracting shared schema types to a foundation zone would eliminate it

The unit/branch-work-analysis zone's low cohesion and mixed content (schema, services, test helpers) suggests it was shaped by import-graph proximity rather than domain intent — manual zone boundary correction is warranted

The sourcevision analyzers package is split across three zones (Analysis Engine, File Analysis Pipeline, Zone Detection Engine) with heavy bidirectional cross-imports totalling 35+ edges — the Louvain algorithm has fragmented a logically cohesive module, and these three zones are strong candidates for consolidation into one or two well-bounded layers.

Zone Detection Engine is the highest-risk zone in this batch: below-threshold cohesion (0.29) and above-threshold coupling (0.71) together mean it is loosely internally connected yet heavily depended upon externally — isolating the Louvain algorithm behind a stable interface would reduce systemic risk.

The Branch Work Analysis zone (unit) is the heaviest importer across the graph (30 imports into Analysis Engine, 3 into Zone Detection Engine) — if unit contains test files that import broadly across the production surface, consider whether some of those imports reveal missing abstractions in the production API.

Schema Validation and Test Fixtures are both perfectly self-contained (cohesion: 1.0, coupling: 0.0), representing the cleanest zones in the codebase; their isolation pattern is worth emulating when designing new modules.

Cross-zone imports flow predominantly from test zones (unit, e2e) into production zones, which is the correct direction; the only bidirectional production-to-production coupling is between the three analyzer zones, reinforcing the consolidation recommendation above.

Three analyzer zones (analyzers, analyzers-2, analyzers-3) alongside the main analysis-engine zone suggests the Louvain algorithm fragmented a single domain — consider consolidating into one zone with explicit submodule structure.
The e2e zone imports from analyzers-2 (3 imports) and unit imports from e2e (2 imports), mixing test and production dependency directions — e2e test zones should only be imported by other test zones.
Workspace root and claude-config zones have zero coupling, which is architecturally correct — config and documentation should never appear in runtime import graphs.

Three algorithmically distinct analyzer zones (analyzers, analyzers-2, analyzers-3) share 30+ cross-zone import edges and likely represent a single logical subsystem fragmented by community detection; a manual zone boundary review targeting these three zones is the highest-value architectural action available.
The fixtures zone has perfect isolation and the schema-validation zone is similarly self-contained — both are positive anchors that demonstrate what well-bounded modules look like in this codebase.
No zone in this batch has critical-severity issues; the primary structural concern is zone fragmentation in the analysis layer rather than any hard coupling violations or circular dependencies.

src/analyzers/ contains files from 5 zones (analysis-engine, branch-work-analysis, cli-operations, file-analysis-pipeline, zone-detection-engine) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

The cross-zone import graph has a single critical layer inversion: analysis-engine (domain) imports 12 symbols from cli-operations (CLI layer); in a clean layered architecture all import edges must point downward — CLI → Domain → Foundation — never upward

schema/index.ts and schema/v1.ts function as an undeclared foundation layer: they are hub nodes imported across 3–4 zones yet live inside branch-work-analysis, meaning there is no zone boundary protecting them; every change to these files has blast radius across the entire import graph without any encapsulation

The test-to-production import direction is correct throughout the graph (unit, e2e import production zones) except for the unit → e2e edge (2 imports): production-adjacent test helpers importing from e2e fixtures inverts the test dependency hierarchy and risks test infrastructure leaking into unit test scope

Zones with cohesion 1.0 (e2e, fixtures) share a common pattern: each is a self-contained leaf with zero coupling; their isolation is not accidental — both serve as read-only data providers (fixtures) or terminal test consumers (e2e) with no upstream dependents in production code, confirming that leaf-node roles naturally produce ideal metrics

The five zones sharing src/analyzers/ as a physical directory (analysis-engine, branch-work-analysis, cli-operations, analyzers-2, analyzers-3) means file location provides no signal about zone membership; this increases the cognitive cost of reasoning about architectural boundaries and makes the layer inversion (analysis-engine → cli) invisible without consulting the import graph

A dependency triangle exists between the three highest-traffic production zones (analysis-engine/analyzers, cli-operations/cli, branch-work-analysis/unit) — all three pairs have bidirectional coupling, forming a tightly coupled triad with no clean layer ordering. Breaking any one bidirectional edge in this triangle would have outsized architectural value.

The schema modules (src/schema/index.ts, src/schema/v1.ts) are the only files in the codebase that behave as a true foundation layer — imported by many zones but importing from none — yet they are not governed as such. Formalizing them as an explicit foundation zone would give other packages a stable, versioned contract surface rather than an implicit import hub.

Test zones (unit, e2e) are the dominant importers across the graph (30+3 imports into production zones) but this is directionally correct — the architectural concern is not the volume but whether the breadth of test imports reveals missing abstractions in the production API surface that tests must work around by importing internals directly.

The three algorithmically fragmented analyzer zones (file-analysis-pipeline, zone-detection-engine, and the main analysis-engine) collectively share 35+ cross-zone import edges but occupy a single physical directory (src/analyzers/) with no subdirectory structure — the directory layout and the zone layout are completely misaligned, which means file-system navigation provides no signal about architectural boundaries.

Zone pairs with clean, unidirectional relationships (e.g., schema-validation ← analyzers, test-fixtures → nothing) represent the positive structural anchors of this codebase; new modules should be designed to attach to these clean anchors rather than to the bidirectionally coupled analyzer triad.

Layer inversion detected: analysis-engine (domain layer) imports 12 symbols from cli-operations (CLI layer); this violates the fundamental constraint that domain code must not depend on its own CLI adapters — remediation requires moving the depended-upon symbols down to a shared utility or types module that both layers can safely import

Schema foundation types (v1.ts, schema/index.ts, data-files.ts) have no dedicated zone; they are cross-cutting hub nodes embedded inside branch-work-analysis, which means the highest-fan-out types in the codebase carry the coupling score of a mixed-responsibility zone rather than the isolation score they deserve — extracting them into a schema-foundation zone is the highest-leverage refactoring available

unit imports from e2e (2 imports): test helper zone importing from end-to-end test zone reverses the expected test dependency direction; e2e fixtures or utilities used by unit tests should be promoted to a shared test-support layer rather than creating an upward dependency into e2e scope

The analyzers, cli, and unit zones form a fully-connected bidirectional triad — every pair of the three has imports flowing in both directions. This is the most critical structural pattern in the codebase because it means there is no layering: any change in any of the three zones can cascade to all others.

The e2e zone imports from analyzers-2 (a production zone), violating the rule that e2e test zones should only import from other test zones or through the public API surface — this is a test-isolation defect that risks coupling end-to-end test stability to internal production module structure.

The three fragmented analyzer zones (analysis-engine, analyzers-2, analyzers-3) contribute 33 bidirectional cross-zone edges among themselves; because these edges cross zone boundaries they inflate coupling scores and obscure the real dependency structure — consolidating into one or two intentionally bounded zones would convert internal cross-zone edges into intra-zone edges, reducing measured coupling without changing any runtime behavior

src/analyzers/ contains files from 5 different zones with no subdirectory separation — the physical file layout provides zero architectural guidance. Adding subdirectories (e.g., src/analyzers/pipeline/, src/analyzers/clustering/) would make zone boundaries visible to developers navigating the codebase.

The 17 bidirectional import edges between analysis-engine and cli-operations (12 + 5) relative to the combined ~65 files in those two zones yields a cross-zone density approaching intra-zone levels — this is a quantitative signal that the zone boundary between these two is artificial and both zones may need to be dissolved and rebuilt around cleaner domain seams

The root cause of the critical layer inversion (analysis-engine → cli-operations) is likely one or two misplaced files (manifest.ts, branch-work-collector.ts) rather than a pervasive architectural failure — targeted file moves may resolve the majority of the 12 offending imports without requiring a broad refactor

No zone in this codebase has an explicit public API surface (single re-export index) that is enforced at the zone level — all cross-zone imports target internal file paths, meaning zone boundaries exist only in the import graph metadata and are invisible during normal development; adding a per-zone index.ts or public.ts would make boundary violations statically detectable

The import graph has a strict topological violation: analysis-engine (domain) → cli-operations (CLI) creates a cycle in the intended layer order. The three zones (analysis-engine, branch-work-analysis, cli-operations) cannot be placed in any valid topological ordering because all three pairs have bidirectional edges — this means the dependency graph has no valid compile-time layer ordering for these three zones, which is the defining characteristic of an unresolvable coupling triad

Files classified as [cli-command] appear in both branch-work-analysis (mcp.ts, workspace.ts) and cli-operations — CLI command files are scattered across two zones with no consistent zone home, meaning the rule 'CLI commands live in cli-operations' is not enforced by the current zone structure

The cli → unit edge (8 imports) has no prior analysis: the CLI layer importing 8 symbols from the branch-work-analysis/unit zone (which mixes schema types, services, and test helpers) means CLI runtime dependencies are unresolvably entangled with a zone of ambiguous classification — if any of these 8 symbols are production schema types they belong in a foundation zone; the current arrangement makes it impossible to audit which CLI imports are production-safe without inspecting every import individually.

The three fragmented analyzer zones (file-analysis-pipeline, zone-detection-engine, and the main analysis-engine) each have an [entrypoint] or [service] file that should define a zone boundary, but the 35+ cross-zone edges prove none of these nominal boundaries is being respected at the import level — the zone structure is aspirational rather than enforced, and no linting or architectural test currently validates it.

The analyzers-3 zone (referenced in cross-imports but not detailed in this batch) participates in bidirectional coupling with both analyzers and unit zones simultaneously; an unnamed zone with bidirectional edges to two other bidirectionally coupled zones closes a four-node coupling cycle (analyzers ↔ analyzers-3 ↔ unit ↔ analyzers) that has not been identified in prior observations — this cycle means no zone in the set can be safely refactored without coordinating changes across all four.

Every zone with cohesion 1.0 in this codebase (schema-validation, test-fixtures) is a leaf node with no outgoing production imports; every zone with cohesion below 0.5 (zone-detection-engine at 0.29, file-analysis-pipeline at 0.48) has heavy bidirectional production imports — this confirms that cohesion degradation in this codebase is driven entirely by cross-zone production coupling, not by internal file disorganization, and that the correct remediation is interface extraction rather than file reorganization.

CLI command files ([cli-command] archetype) are distributed across both branch-work-analysis and cli-operations zones with no enforced zone rule; developers adding new CLI commands have no reliable signal about which zone to target, causing continued fragmentation — establish a single canonical zone for all CLI command files

The analysis-engine, branch-work-analysis, and cli-operations zones form a fully-connected bidirectional import triad with no valid topological ordering — this is not a coupling smell but a structural deadlock: no layering scheme can be enforced while all three pairs remain bidirectionally coupled; at least one bidirectional edge must be eliminated before any layer rule can be statically enforced

Zero zones in this codebase expose a single-file public API surface (index.ts or public.ts at the zone level); all cross-zone imports target arbitrary internal file paths, making it impossible to detect boundary violations statically — each zone should define and enforce a single canonical export surface to make the import graph auditable without tooling

cli → unit: 8 imports represents the CLI layer taking a runtime dependency on a zone whose files include test helpers and schema types with no encapsulation boundary; without knowing which of the 8 symbols are production-safe, the CLI's true dependency surface cannot be audited — the unit zone must be split into a production schema/types subzone and a test-support subzone before this edge can be classified as acceptable or critical.

A four-node bidirectional coupling cycle exists across analyzers ↔ analyzers-3 ↔ unit ↔ analyzers (closing through the analyzers-3 zone visible only in the cross-import table); cycles of this size mean the effective blast radius of any change in these zones is the union of all four zone surfaces — this is the widest unmitigated coupling risk in the codebase.

The short aliases used in the cross-zone import table (unit, cli, analyzers, analyzers-2, analyzers-3) do not match the canonical zone IDs (branch-work-analysis, cli-operations, analysis-engine); this alias drift means the import table cannot be joined with zone metadata without a manual alias map, reducing tooling reliability

The three algorithmically named zones (analyzers-2, analyzers-3, and the partially renamed analysis-engine) have no semantic zone names derived from their actual domain content — opaque auto-generated IDs persist in the canonical zone registry, making architectural discussions ambiguous and blocking meaningful zone-level ownership assignment

Every zone in this codebase with cohesion below 0.5 (branch-work-analysis: 0.42, analyzers-3: 0.29, analyzers-2: 0.48) also participates in at least one bidirectional coupling pair — the data confirms that low cohesion and bidirectional coupling are co-occurring symptoms of the same root cause (zone boundaries drawn by import proximity rather than domain intent), not independent problems

No zone in this codebase has an explicit owner or team annotation; combined with no enforced public API surfaces, this means there is no human-level accountability to match the absence of machine-level enforcement — adding ownership metadata per zone would create a social contract that complements any future linting rules

The four zones with files inside src/analyzers/ (analysis-engine, branch-work-analysis, cli-operations, and the unnamed analyzers-2/3 zones) collectively represent a single physical directory that is architecturally ungoverned; the absence of subdirectories means a developer creating a new file in src/analyzers/ has no filesystem signal about which zone it belongs to, guaranteeing continued zone boundary erosion

The bidirectional coupling between file-analysis-pipeline and analysis-engine (15 + 7 = 22 edges) means the 'pipeline' metaphor encoded in the zone name is architecturally false — a true pipeline stage has unidirectional data flow; bidirectional import edges between adjacent pipeline stages indicate shared mutable state or circular orchestration that the pipeline abstraction was intended to prevent.

The cross-zone import table shows analyzers-3 importing from both analyzers (11 edges) and unit (3 edges) simultaneously — this third analyzer fragment acts as a bridge node that couples two already bidirectionally-coupled zones together, closing a four-node cycle. Bridge nodes are the highest-leverage extraction targets because removing the bridge severs multiple coupling edges in a single refactor.

No zone in this batch exposes a versioned or tagged public API contract. The schema types in src/schema/v1.ts carry a version suffix ('v1') implying a versioning intention, but no other file or zone follows this convention — establish a consistent versioning policy for the schema foundation layer so that v2 changes can be introduced without breaking all 3-4 importing zones simultaneously.

The analyzers-3 zone (visible only via cross-import edges, not detailed in zone metadata) participates in bidirectional coupling with two zones that are themselves bidirectionally coupled — it is the most invisible high-risk node in the codebase because it appears in the import table but not in zone details, making its file composition and cohesion metrics inaccessible without a dedicated zone analysis pass.

All zones with cohesion ≥ 0.9 (schema-validation, test-fixtures) have zero outgoing production imports; all zones with cohesion ≤ 0.5 (zone-detection-engine, file-analysis-pipeline) have ≥ 10 outgoing production imports. This inverse correlation is monotonic across all four zones in this batch, confirming that in this codebase cohesion degradation is entirely explained by cross-zone production coupling volume — no zone has low cohesion from internal disorganization alone.

The cross-zone import table lists 15 distinct directed edges but the 'other zones' note references cli, analyzers, and unit zones with 26-39 files each — the three largest zones by file count are absent from the detailed zone analysis in this batch, meaning the zones with the most files and the highest cross-zone import traffic have received the least per-zone insight depth. Prioritize detailed analysis of the analyzers (39 files), cli (26 files), and unit (29 files) zones as they are the dominant structural risk nodes.

Establish a zone alias registry that maps short import-table aliases (unit, cli, analyzers) to canonical zone IDs (branch-work-analysis, cli-operations, analysis-engine); without this mapping, cross-analysis-pass comparisons silently fail when an alias changes but the canonical ID does not

Rename algorithmically generated zone IDs (analyzers-2, analyzers-3) to semantically meaningful names derived from their dominant file responsibilities before adding new files; opaque IDs make zone ownership undiscoverable and block meaningful architectural governance

Add per-zone ownership annotations (team or file-owner) to the zone registry; the combination of no enforced public API, no ownership, and no linting means zone boundaries exist only as metadata with zero enforcement at any level — ownership is the lowest-cost first enforcement mechanism available

The 'pipeline' naming convention for file-analysis-pipeline is architecturally misleading given its 22 bidirectional cross-zone import edges with analysis-engine — reserve the 'pipeline' zone naming convention exclusively for zones with verified unidirectional data flow, and rename or dissolve zones that violate it to prevent the metaphor from providing false architectural confidence.

analyzers-3 zone appears in the cross-import table but has no zone detail entry — any zone visible in the import graph but absent from zone metadata is an analysis blind spot. Ensure every zone with cross-zone import edges has a corresponding metadata entry with cohesion, coupling, and file list before architectural recommendations are finalized.

src/schema/v1.ts uses a version suffix implying a schema evolution strategy, but no other file or zone applies version tagging and there is no migration or compatibility policy documented — either enforce the versioning convention across all schema exports or remove the v1 suffix to avoid implying a versioning contract that does not exist.

The three largest zones by file count (analyzers: 39, cli: 26, unit: 29) account for the majority of cross-zone import traffic but have not been included in the detailed zone analysis batch — architectural recommendations derived from the four smaller zones in this batch may be invalidated or reordered once the three hub zones are analyzed; defer finalizing remediation priorities until all hub zones are characterized.

[call graph] 187 calls: "branch-work-analysis" → "analysis-engine"
[call graph] 74 calls: "analysis-engine" → "cli-operations"

findings.text: 16 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
16 entry points is unusually broad for a single zone; audit whether all exports need to be public or if the public API surface can be narrowed.
High cohesion (0.66) and low coupling (0.34) indicate a well-bounded analysis domain — this is the healthiest zone in the batch.
claude-client.ts should proxy through @n-dx/llm-client to maintain the four-tier dependency hierarchy and avoid direct vendor coupling.
High coupling (0.58) — 30 imports target "analysis-engine"
Coupling at 0.58 approaches the warning threshold (>0.6); monitor as new consumers of schema/v1.ts and data-files.ts are added.
Extracting schema/v1.ts and schema/data-files.ts into a dedicated schema-foundation zone would reduce coupling and clarify the dependency graph for downstream consumers.
The zone mixes schema foundation files (v1.ts, data-files.ts), branch-work services, and a test helper — low cohesion (0.42) reflects this mixed responsibility.
Isolated files — no import edges between 2 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Zone contains only editor configuration — zero cohesion and coupling scores are expected and correct for this role.
settings.local.json may contain developer-specific overrides that shouldn't be committed; verify .gitignore excludes it.
High coupling (0.52) — 15 imports target "analysis-engine"

Bidirectional coupling with the Analysis Engine zone (analyzers-2 → analyzers: 15, analyzers → analyzers-2: 7) indicates the algorithmic boundary between these two zones does not match the intended module boundary — consider treating them as one cohesive analysis layer.

Cohesion (0.48) and coupling (0.52) are both near the midpoint, reflecting a zone that is neither fully self-contained nor excessively open — acceptable for a pipeline stage but worth monitoring as the analyzer count grows.

src/util/paths.ts is listed as an entrypoint, suggesting it is imported by multiple external zones; if path utilities are widely shared, they may belong in a dedicated utility zone rather than alongside the analyzer services.
Bidirectional coupling: "analysis-engine" ↔ "branch-work-analysis" (7+30 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Hub: src/analyzers/zones.ts is imported by 4 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Size imbalance: largest zone has 39 files vs smallest with 2 — uneven decomposition
Fan-in hotspot: src/cli/output.ts receives calls from 13 files (high fan-in expected for this module type) — high-impact module, changes may have wide ripple effects
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

The algorithmic zone ID 'e2e' was assigned because the e2e test files dominate by count; ensure CI tooling and zone-based filtering do not inadvertently exclude validate.ts from production code analysis passes.
Zero external coupling means the schema validator can be evolved or replaced without impacting any other zone at runtime — a desirable property worth preserving as schema versions are added.

src/schema/validate.ts is the sole production file in this zone alongside three test files spanning all test levels — this is a well-tested, isolated module and a positive example of the single-responsibility principle.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

Both fixture projects are realistic enough to exercise route detection, import graph construction, and component cataloging — maintaining at least one framework-based and one plain-TS fixture as the test suite grows will keep coverage representative.

The Remix fixture includes nested route files (_auth.login.tsx, users.$id.tsx) which are valuable for validating file-system route detection; ensure the route-detection analyzer tests reference this fixture explicitly rather than synthetic data.
Zero coupling confirms no production file imports from fixtures, which is correct; any accidental runtime import of fixture data would be a test-isolation defect to address immediately.
Isolated files — no import edges between 10 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Multiple architecture documentation files (ARCHITECTURE.md, WORKSPACE_DESIGN.md, README.md) at the root provide good onboarding context for the monorepo.
Zero coupling is expected here — root config files should not be imported by source code.
tsconfig.tsbuildinfo is a build artifact and should be added to .gitignore to avoid noisy diffs.
High coupling (0.71) — 11 imports target "analysis-engine"
Low cohesion (0.29) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone

Cohesion of 0.29 is below the 0.4 threshold, indicating that the files grouped here have sparse internal import connections relative to their external ones — zone-hash.ts in particular may be imported more by consumers than by its zone peers.

Coupling of 0.71 exceeds the 0.6 warning threshold; combined with bidirectional imports to both the Analysis Engine and Branch Work Analysis zones, the zone detection subsystem is a high-traffic hub that could become a bottleneck for future refactors.

The Louvain algorithm (louvain.ts) and the zone lifecycle manager (zones.ts) are co-located but serve distinct concerns — algorithm versus orchestration; splitting them into separate modules would improve testability and lower coupling.
Zone "analysis-engine" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The zone name 'analysis-engine' is singular and bounded but the zone spans 9 directories with 16 entry points; rename or split the zone so the name truthfully describes its scope — a misleading name causes developers to underestimate blast radius when modifying files here

completion-reader.ts handles streaming completion I/O while its sibling files perform domain analysis — extract streaming/I/O adapters into a dedicated adapter layer below the analysis services to give I/O concerns a stable, separately evolvable home
Zone "branch-work-analysis" has files across 11 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The 'unit' alias used in cross-import tables for this zone is a residual name from a prior zone structure; update all documentation and tooling references to use the canonical ID 'branch-work-analysis' to prevent import tracing errors in future analysis passes

branch-work-analysis is the only zone with both cohesion below 0.5 and coupling above 0.5 — treat it as the highest-fragility zone in the codebase; prioritize extracting schema foundation types first (reduces coupling) and moving CLI artifacts out second (increases cohesion) before adding any new files to this zone

workspace-aggregate.ts and workspace.ts (CLI) share a naming prefix implying a module family, but are governed by different zone boundaries with no enforced convention; document an explicit naming rule — domain aggregates use '-aggregate' suffix, CLI adapters use the bare noun — and verify all paired domain/CLI files follow it
Zone "cli-operations" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

export-pdf.ts introduces a binary rendering dependency (PDF) into cli-operations alongside text-output commands; if PDF generation pulls in a heavyweight renderer dependency, extract it to a separate output-renderers zone to prevent that dependency from being loaded for all CLI consumers that never use PDF export

git-credential-helper.ts handles credential secrets inside the same zone as general CLI output commands; isolate it behind a CredentialHelper interface or move it to a dedicated security-utilities zone so that secret-handling logic has an explicit, auditable boundary and cannot be accidentally imported by non-security callers

src/analyzers/ contains files from 5 zones (analysis-engine, branch-work-analysis, cli-operations, file-analysis-pipeline, zone-detection-engine) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

Add per-zone ownership annotations (team or file-owner) to the zone registry; the combination of no enforced public API, no ownership, and no linting means zone boundaries exist only as metadata with zero enforcement at any level — ownership is the lowest-cost first enforcement mechanism available

Establish a zone alias registry that maps short import-table aliases (unit, cli, analyzers) to canonical zone IDs (branch-work-analysis, cli-operations, analysis-engine); without this mapping, cross-analysis-pass comparisons silently fail when an alias changes but the canonical ID does not

Rename algorithmically generated zone IDs (analyzers-2, analyzers-3) to semantically meaningful names derived from their dominant file responsibilities before adding new files; opaque IDs make zone ownership undiscoverable and block meaningful architectural governance

The 'pipeline' naming convention for file-analysis-pipeline is architecturally misleading given its 22 bidirectional cross-zone import edges with analysis-engine — reserve the 'pipeline' zone naming convention exclusively for zones with verified unidirectional data flow, and rename or dissolve zones that violate it to prevent the metaphor from providing false architectural confidence.

The three largest zones by file count (analyzers: 39, cli: 26, unit: 29) account for the majority of cross-zone import traffic but have not been included in the detailed zone analysis batch — architectural recommendations derived from the four smaller zones in this batch may be invalidated or reordered once the three hub zones are analyzed; defer finalizing remediation priorities until all hub zones are characterized.

analyzers-3 zone appears in the cross-import table but has no zone detail entry — any zone visible in the import graph but absent from zone metadata is an analysis blind spot. Ensure every zone with cross-zone import edges has a corresponding metadata entry with cohesion, coupling, and file list before architectural recommendations are finalized.

src/schema/v1.ts uses a version suffix implying a schema evolution strategy, but no other file or zone applies version tagging and there is no migration or compatibility policy documented — either enforce the versioning convention across all schema exports or remove the v1 suffix to avoid implying a versioning contract that does not exist.
Hub function: info in src/cli/output.ts is called from 9 files (high fan-in expected for this module type) — changes here have wide impact, consider if responsibilities can be narrowed
Potentially unused export: detectBranchLifecycle in src/analyzers/branch-work-filter.ts has no incoming calls — verify if still needed
Potentially unused export: resolveWorkedEpicTitlesForRange in src/cli/commands/prd-epic-resolver.ts has no incoming calls — verify if still needed
Zone "test-fixtures" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Add a fixture integrity test that asserts the expected internal import graph of small-ts-project — if fixture files are accidentally modified, import graph tests may silently pass with stale expected data rather than failing with a clear fixture-changed error.

Document the Remix fixture's file-system routing convention dependency (Remix v2 _prefix and $ param syntax) in a fixture README or inline comment — undocumented fixture conventions break silently when route-detection heuristics are updated for a different router.
Bidirectional imports between cli and unit zones (8 + 4 cross-zone imports) indicate a cycle risk; shared types or utilities should be extracted downward.
High cohesion (0.67) confirms CLI commands are correctly organized around shared output and error infrastructure.
prd-epic-resolver.ts references PRD/epic concepts inside the sourcevision CLI zone — verify this doesn't create a hidden runtime dependency on rex.

Schema foundation types (v1.ts, schema/index.ts) are co-located inside branch-work-analysis rather than a dedicated foundation zone; consumers importing schema types inadvertently couple to the branch-work-analysis zone boundary, making schema extraction riskier over time

prd-epic-resolver.ts references PRD/epic domain concepts inside the sourcevision CLI zone; audit whether this file imports from rex at runtime, which would violate the four-tier dependency hierarchy and the gateway pattern without a declared gateway module

src/schema/index.ts and src/schema/v1.ts function as a shared foundation layer (imported by 3–4 zones, zero outgoing imports) but are classified under a zone named 'schema-validation' dominated by test files — rename or restructure to reflect the foundation role and prevent the schema types from being treated as validation-layer internals.

Fixture projects spanning 5 directories with no centralized entry point risk being partially excluded by glob-based ignore rules — verify that sourcevision's own manifest ignore patterns do not accidentally suppress fixture files needed for e2e test coverage.

Zone-detection-engine has the worst cohesion/coupling ratio (0.29/0.71) at the smallest meaningful size (6 files) — this combination makes it the highest per-file refactoring risk in the codebase and the most urgent candidate for boundary stabilization.

analysis-engine → cli-operations (12 imports): domain layer importing from CLI layer is a hard layer inversion; analysis services must not depend on CLI command modules — extract shared types or utilities downward to break this coupling

branch-work-analysis ↔ analysis-engine (30+7 crossings) is the dominant edge pair in the graph — 37 cross-zone imports between two zones signals that zone boundaries do not reflect real module seams; these zones likely share a common abstraction that should be extracted

cli-operations receives 12 imports from analysis-engine while also sending 5 imports back — bidirectional coupling with a domain layer means cli-operations contains logic that the domain needs, which should instead live in a shared utility or types layer

src/util/paths.ts should be extracted to a dedicated utility zone; its presence in the file-analysis-pipeline zone obscures its true role as a cross-cutting shared utility and inflates this zone's apparent coupling.

16 entry points spanning 9 directories means there is no canonical import path into this zone; consumers import from arbitrary internal paths, making any file move a breaking change — consolidate exports behind a single index or public.ts to create a stable zone API surface

enrich-config.ts (classified [config]) is co-located with domain services in analysis-engine; configuration files that change on deployment cadence should not share a zone with algorithmic services that change on feature cadence — extract to a dedicated config layer to decouple change rates

mcp.ts is a CLI command co-located with domain services in branch-work-analysis; CLI adapters must not share a zone boundary with domain logic they adapt — move mcp.ts to cli-operations to enforce CLI-above-domain layering within the zone, not just across zones

pr-markdown-template.ts is a presentation-layer template embedded in a domain analysis zone; output templates have no domain responsibility and should reside in cli-operations or a dedicated output-rendering zone to prevent domain logic from acquiring a dependency on formatting details

branch-work-collector.ts lives in cli-operations but collects domain data for branch-work-analysis; moving it to branch-work-analysis would eliminate a cross-layer import forcing analysis-engine to reach into the CLI zone for domain data collection logic

manifest.ts is an analysis pipeline artifact (analysis-engine output) placed inside cli-operations; if analysis-engine imports it, this single misplacement is a direct cause of the critical layer inversion — move manifest.ts into analysis-engine or a shared types layer

src/analyzers/index.ts is designated [entrypoint] but the zone still exports 15 cross-zone import edges to analysis-engine, meaning the barrel export is not functioning as a stable abstraction boundary — consumers import implementation files directly, rendering the entrypoint ineffective as an isolation mechanism.

src/analyzers/route-detection.ts carries the [route-handler] archetype but performs static file-path analysis, not HTTP routing; this archetype mislabel causes tooling to misclassify a static analysis utility as an application-layer request handler — reclassify to [utility].

tests/e2e/cli-analyze.test.ts and tests/e2e/cli-serve.test.ts are CLI behavior tests misclassified into the schema-validation zone because they happen to import schema types; this makes the schema zone appear to own CLI e2e coverage and splits CLI test files across two zones — move both files to the CLI e2e test zone.

zone-hash.ts generates stable zone IDs consumed by both zone detection and output generation phases; enclosing it inside the zone-detection-engine zone forces output writers to import across a zone boundary into the detection subsystem rather than from a shared utility layer — extract to a dedicated utility zone.

Three archetypes ([service], [utility], [route-handler]) coexist in this zone with no documented rule governing which archetype belongs here — establish an explicit archetype policy per zone to make misclassifications detectable by tooling.

src/analyzers/index.ts barrel export should be either lint-enforced as the only allowed cross-zone import point or deleted — a barrel that consumers bypass provides false encapsulation and adds indirection without isolation benefit.

Create a dedicated cli-e2e zone and move tests/e2e/cli-analyze.test.ts and tests/e2e/cli-serve.test.ts into it — without a canonical CLI e2e zone, zone-level test coverage reports cannot surface CLI end-to-end test presence, and new CLI e2e tests have no clear zone home.

Add a dedicated unit test for the Louvain clustering algorithm in isolation (louvain.test.ts or graph-clustering.test.ts) — the current test suite exercises zones.ts integration but provides no regression harness if the clustering algorithm is swapped or parameterized differently.

Rename louvain.ts to graph-clustering.ts or community-detection.ts to align with the functional naming convention used by all peer files in src/analyzers/ — algorithm names leak implementation details and become stale when the algorithm changes.
